title: Train Travel in Italy
text:
Italian trains are efficient, comfortable, and one of the best ways to travel between cities. Our trip involves three major train journeys, all bookable through Trenitalia or Italo (Italy’s two main rail operators).
Key Routes: 1. Florence → Naples (June 10): Italo high-speed #8902, 3 hours — BOOKED (ticket PIDCMA) 2. Naples → Brindisi (June 13): Via flight — AZ 1270 NAP→FCO + AZ 1623 FCO→BDS (see Flights)

Overview
This is one of Italy’s premier high-speed train routes — fast, comfortable, and scenic.
Booked: Italo #8902, ticket code PIDCMA Departs: Firenze S.M. 12:28pm → Arrives Napoli Centrale 3:28pm Car: Salotto 1 (Lounge) — Seats: Jane (1), Dan (2) Duration: 3 hours even Stations: Firenze SMN → Napoli Centrale

Recommendation: Book Economy or Business class 2-4 weeks ahead for best pricing.


What to Expect
Frecciarossa Features: - Modern, clean trains - Comfortable reclining seats - Power outlets at seats - WiFi (sometimes unreliable) - Quiet cars available - Café/bar car - Luggage storage areas
Seat Selection: - Window seats for views of Italian countryside - Table seats if traveling with companion - Solo seats (1+2 configuration in some cars)


Scenic Highlights
The route passes through: - Tuscan countryside - Apennine mountains - Approaches to Naples with Vesuvius views
Best Views: Sit on the right side (west) approaching Naples for potential Vesuvius sighting.

Napoli Centrale - Main Naples station - Can be chaotic - watch belongings - Taxi stand outside - Metro connections - Hotel pickups coordinate here

Key Decision: Intercity (IC) is the clear winner — direct service, First Class available, and no slow regional connections required.


Is It a Milk Run? First Class Options
Short answer: Take the Intercity (IC) train — it’s NOT a milk run and has First Class seating.

Regional leg IS slow (many stops)



Why Intercity (IC) is the right choice: - Runs the full Naples → Brindisi route without changing trains - Makes major stops only (Foggia, Bari, coastal towns) — not every tiny station - First Class on IC trains: reserved seat, more space, dedicated car, quieter - IC trains are not Frecciarossa-class but they are proper express trains - Fares: €15–40 First Class depending on advance booking - Book at trenitalia.com — search Naples Centrale → Brindisi, filter by IC
What makes the “milk run” concern valid: The Bari→Brindisi regional (Regionale) connection, if you take the Frecciarossa-then-change-at-Bari approach, IS slow — it stops at many coastal towns. That’s the connection to avoid. The IC bypasses this entirely.
Booking tip: IC trains must be booked on Trenitalia (Italo doesn’t serve this southern route). Book 2–4 weeks ahead for best fares and seat selection.


Understanding Puglia Geography
Important: Brindisi is IN Puglia, not before it. The train journey IS a tour through Puglia:
Route Path (North to South): 1. Caserta - Royal Palace (Reggia di Caserta), visible from station 2. Benevento - Roman Arch of Trajan 3. Foggia - Enter Puglia here (gateway to Gargano Peninsula) 4. Bari - Puglia’s capital (~3.5h from Naples) 5. Polignano a Mare - Dramatic cliffside town 6. Ostuni - “White City” on hilltop 7. Brindisi - Historic port city (~5.5h from Naples) 8. Lecce - “Florence of the South” (~6h from Naples, 30 min beyond Brindisi)


Recommended Train
Intercity (IC) Naples Centrale → Brindisi - Morning departure recommended (arrive Brindisi early afternoon) - Check current schedules at trenitalia.com — schedules change seasonally - First Class car: look for “Prima Classe” designation when booking - Journey time: approximately 5h 30min direct
Departure Times: - Morning Options: Earliest ~8:12 AM, Latest ~6:12 PM - Strategy: Take morning train to maximize daylight views through Puglia


Scenic Journey Highlights
What You’ll See: - Crossing the Apennines (mountains) - Entering the Tavoliere delle Puglie (plains) - Adriatic coastline (sit on LEFT side after Foggia) - Olive groves stretching to horizon - Whitewashed towns on hillsides


Recommended Approach
Option A: Direct to Brindisi (Simple) - Morning Frecce train from Naples - Arrive Brindisi early afternoon (~5.5 hours) - Meet tour group or check into hotel - One journey, minimal hassle
Option B: Stop in Bari (Recommended if Time Allows) - Take morning train to Bari (3.5h) - Spend afternoon/evening in Bari Vecchia (old town) - Watch orecchiette ladies making pasta - Visit Basilica di San Nicola - Fresh seafood dinner along the port - Next morning: Easy 1-1.5 hour regional train to Brindisi
Bari Benefits: - Breaks up long journey - Taste of Puglia before tour begins - Beautiful old town (Bari Vecchia) - Day trip options: Matera (cave dwellings, 1h), Alberobello (trulli houses, 1.5h), Polignano a Mare (cliffs, 30 min)

title: Train Travel in Italy
text:
General Train Travel Tips

Booking Strategy
When to Book: - High-speed trains: 2-4 weeks ahead for Super Economy fares - Regional trains: Can buy same day (no reservations)
Where to Book: - Trenitalia.com or Italotreno.it (direct booking best) - Omio, Rail Europe (third-party, often higher fees) - Station ticket windows (if you prefer in-person)


At the Station
Arrival Time: - High-speed trains: Arrive 15-20 minutes early - Regional trains: Arrive 10 minutes early - Check platform (binario) on departure boards
Validating Tickets: - E-tickets (QR code): No validation needed, show on phone - Paper tickets: MUST validate in yellow machines before boarding - Failure to validate = fine from conductor


On the Train
Luggage: - Store large bags in designated racks (near doors or overhead) - Keep valuables with you - Watch bags during stops
Seats: - High-speed: Assigned seats (on ticket) - Regional: Open seating (first-come, first-served)
Quiet Cars: - Some trains have “Silenzio” cars (no phone calls, quiet conversations)


Train Classes Compared
Trenitalia Classes: - Standard/2nd Class: Comfortable, affordable - Premium: More space, quieter - Business: Leather seats, welcome drink, snacks - Executive: Most spacious, full meal service (rare on our routes)
Italo Classes: - Smart: Economy - Comfort: Business equivalent - Prima: First class equivalent

title: Restaurant Recommendations
text: Italian dining is an experience to savor. Key tips: - Reservations: Essential for popular spots, especially dinner - Timing: Lunch 12:30-2:30 PM, Dinner 7:30-10 PM (Italians eat late) - Cover charge (coperto): €1-3 per person is normal - Service: Often slow by U.S. standards - this is intentional - Tipping: Round up or 5-10%, not 15-20%

title: Restaurant Recommendations
text:
General Italy Dining Tips

Meal Structure
Italian meals have courses: 1. Antipasto - Appetizer 2. Primo - First course (pasta, risotto, soup) 3. Secondo - Second course (meat or fish) 4. Contorno - Side vegetable (ordered separately) 5. Dolce - Dessert 6. Caffè - Espresso
You don’t have to order everything! Common to order: - Primo + contorno - OR Secondo + contorno - OR Antipasto + primo


Ordering
Cover Charge (Coperto): €1-3 per person, for bread and table service Service Charge: Sometimes included (servizio incluso) - check bill Water: Ask for “acqua naturale” (still) or “frizzante” (sparkling) Wine: House wine (vino della casa) often excellent and cheap


Tipping

Not expected like in U.S.
Round up bill or leave 5-10% for excellent service
Never 15-20%



Coffee Culture
Stand at the bar: Cheapest (€1-1.50 for espresso) Sit at table: 2-3x more expensive
Types: - Espresso (caffè) - Standard coffee - Cappuccino - Only acceptable before 11 AM - Caffè macchiato - Espresso with dash of milk - Caffè americano - Espresso with hot water (watered-down to Americans)
Never order: Cappuccino after a meal (Italians will judge)

Dietary Needs

Vegetarian

Easy in Italy (pasta, pizza, vegetables)
Say: “Sono vegetariano/a”



Vegan

Harder but possible
Say: “Sono vegano/a, senza latte e uova”
Many pastas naturally vegan



Gluten-Free

Common (celiac disease awareness high in Italy)
Say: “Sono celiaco/a”
Look for “senza glutine” on menus



Food Allergies

Take seriously in Italy
Write out allergy in Italian
Show to server

title: Museums & Attractions
text:
Many Italian museums require advance booking, especially Florence’s major art museums. Book 2-3 months ahead for June travel.
Consider: Firenze Card (€85/72 hours) covers most museums + skip-the-line access.

title: Museums & Attractions
text:
Florence Museums

Uffizi Gallery ⭐ PRIORITY BOOKING
Why Visit: Home to the world’s finest Renaissance art collection
Masterpieces: - Botticelli’s Birth of Venus and Primavera - Leonardo da Vinci’s Annunciation - Michelangelo’s Doni Tondo - Caravaggio’s Medusa - Raphael, Titian, and countless others
Practical Information: - Booking: ESSENTIAL - Book 2-3 months ahead - Website: uffizi.it - Price: €20-€25 (includes temporary exhibitions) - Time Needed: 2.5-4 hours - Best Time: First entry (8:15 AM) or late afternoon - Closed: Mondays
Tips: - Book timed entry slot - Arrive 15 min early with printed confirmation - Start on 2nd floor, work chronologically - Don’t miss Botticelli rooms (10-14) - Café terrace has Duomo views



Galleria dell’Accademia (David) ⭐ PRIORITY BOOKING
Why Visit: Michelangelo’s David statue
Highlights: - David - The iconic 17-foot marble masterpiece - Michelangelo’s Prisoners (unfinished sculptures showing his process) - Plaster models and studies - Musical instrument collection
Practical Information: - Booking: ESSENTIAL - Sells out weeks ahead - Website: galleriaaccademiafirenze.it - Price: €12-€16 - Time Needed: 1-1.5 hours - Best Time: First entry (8:15 AM) to see David with fewer crowds - Closed: Mondays
Tips: - Smaller museum than Uffizi - quicker visit - David is in the Tribune gallery (can’t miss it) - Get there early - crowds gather by mid-morning - Photography allowed (no flash)



Duomo Complex (Cathedral, Dome, Baptistery, Bell Tower)
What’s Included: - Cathedral (free entry to nave) - Brunelleschi’s Dome climb - Giotto’s Bell Tower (Campanile) - Baptistery (Ghiberti’s “Gates of Paradise”) - Opera Museum - Crypt
Booking: - Dome Climb: MUST BOOK - Limited capacity, timed entry - Website: duomo.firenze.it - Price: €30 (combo ticket for all sites, valid 3 days) - Dome Climb: 463 steps, no elevator, narrow passages - Time: 2-3 hours for all sites - Closed: Sundays (religious services)
Dome Climb Tips: - Book earliest available time - Not for claustrophobic or mobility-limited - Incredible 360° Florence views from top - See Vasari’s frescoes up close



Bargello Museum
Why Visit: World’s best Renaissance sculpture collection
Highlights: - Donatello’s bronze David (first nude sculpture since antiquity) - Michelangelo’s early works - Della Robbia ceramics - Verrocchio sculptures
Practical Information: - Booking: Recommended but not always essential - Website: bargellomusei.beniculturali.it - Price: €9-€11 - Time Needed: 1.5-2 hours - Best Time: Afternoons (less crowded) - Closed: 2nd and 4th Mondays
Tips: - Often overlooked - fewer crowds - Start on ground floor, work up - Don’t miss Donatello room (first floor)



Medici Chapels
Why Visit: Michelangelo’s sculptural tombs for the Medici family
Highlights: - Tomb of Lorenzo de’ Medici with Dawn and Dusk - Tomb of Giuliano de’ Medici with Day and Night - Cappella dei Principi (Chapel of Princes) - opulent marble - Medici family tombs
Practical Information: - Booking: Recommended - Website: bargellomusei.beniculturali.it - Price: €9 - Time Needed: 45-60 minutes - Closed: Mondays



Palazzo Pitti & Boboli Gardens
What’s Included: - Palatine Gallery (Raphael, Titian paintings) - Royal Apartments - Modern Art Gallery - Costume Gallery - Boboli Gardens (16th-century formal gardens)
Practical Information: - Booking: Recommended for peak times - Website: uffizi.it - Price: €16 (palace + gardens) - Time Needed: 2-3 hours (more if leisurely garden stroll) - Best Time: Morning for palace, afternoon for gardens
Tips: - Gardens are extensive - wear comfortable shoes - Great escape from museum crowds - Café in gardens for break



Santa Croce
Why Visit: Franciscan church with tombs of famous Florentines
Highlights: - Tombs of Michelangelo, Galileo, Machiavelli, Rossini - Giotto frescoes (Bardi and Peruzzi Chapels) - Pazzi Chapel by Brunelleschi - Leather school (free to enter from side)
Practical Information: - Booking: Not required - Price: €8 - Time Needed: 45-60 minutes - Dress Code: Shoulders and knees covered



Firenze Card
What Is It: €85 pass valid 72 hours
Includes: - Uffizi, Accademia, Palazzo Pitti, Bargello, Medici Chapels - 60+ museums and sites - Skip-the-line access (reserve time slots)
Worth It? - YES if visiting 4+ major museums in 3 days - NO if leisurely pace (2-3 museums)
Calculate: €20 (Uffizi) + €16 (Accademia) + €16 (Pitti) + €9 (Bargello) = €61 - Card pays for itself with 4 museums - Bonus: Skip lines, flexibility
Buy: firenzecard.it

title: Museums & Attractions
text:
Naples Museums

MANN (Museo Archeologico Nazionale di Napoli) ⭐ PRIORITY
Why Visit: World’s finest Roman artifacts, most from Pompeii & Herculaneum
Collections: - Pompeii and Herculaneum artifacts - Farnese Collection (massive Roman sculptures) - Alexander Mosaic (from Pompeii) - Secret Cabinet (erotic Roman art) - Egyptian collection - Coin and medal collection
Practical Information: - Booking: Recommended (can buy at door but may have lines) - Website: museoarcheologiconapoli.it - Price: €18 - Time Needed: 2-3 hours (could spend full day) - Best Time: Morning - Closed: Tuesdays
Tips: - Go AFTER Herculaneum visit (context makes artifacts meaningful) - Don’t miss Secret Cabinet (room VII) - Huge museum - pace yourself - Café for breaks



Cappella Sansevero ⭐ MUST SEE
Why Visit: The Veiled Christ - one of the world’s most astonishing sculptures
Highlights: - Veiled Christ by Giuseppe Sanmartino (1753) - marble veil appears translucent - Disillusion by Francesco Queirolo - fisherman’s net carved in marble - Modesty by Antonio Corradini - Ceiling fresco Glory of Paradise
Practical Information: - Booking: ESSENTIAL - Timed entry, very small chapel - Website: museosansevero.it - Price: €9 - Time Needed: 30-45 minutes - Photography: NOT allowed - Closed: Tuesdays
Tips: - Book earliest slot available - Tiny chapel - gets very crowded - Stand directly under the Veiled Christ - Worth the booking hassle - unforgettable



Other Naples Sites
Napoli Sotterranea (Underground Naples) - 40 meters below street level - Ancient Roman theater, WWII shelters - Greek aqueducts - Guided tours only - Book at napolisotterranea.org
Castel Nuovo (Maschio Angioino) - Medieval castle - Civic Museum - Great views
Palazzo Reale di Napoli - Royal Palace - Lavish apartments - Throne room

title: Museums & Attractions
text:
General Museum Tips

Booking Strategy
Priority 1 (Book First): 1. Uffizi Gallery 2. Accademia (David) 3. Duomo Dome Climb 4. Cappella Sansevero
Priority 2 (Book Soon After): 5. MANN Naples 6. Palazzo Pitti 7. Bargello 8. Medici Chapels


Timing Your Museum Days
Don’t Over-Schedule: - Max 2 major museums per day - Morning museum + afternoon lighter activity - Build in rest/wandering time
Energy Management: - Start with high-priority museums early in trip - Alternate museum days with outdoor days - Save smaller museums for when fatigued


At the Museum
Arrival: - Arrive 15 minutes early with confirmation - Have ID matching booking name - Print confirmations (don’t rely on phone)
Inside: - Ask for museum map - Identify “must-see” pieces first - Take breaks (museum fatigue is real) - Photography rules vary (usually no flash, sometimes no photos)
Crowds: - Worst: 11 AM - 2 PM - Best: First entry (8:15 AM) or after 4 PM - Avoid weekends if possible

Location Priorities
Best Neighborhoods:
1. Santa Maria Novella (Near SMN Station) - Walking distance from train arrival - Central to major sites - Good restaurant options - Can be touristy
2. San Lorenzo / Mercato Centrale - Authentic neighborhood - Great food markets - Near Medici Chapels - Less touristy than Duomo area

4. Santa Croce - Vibrant piazza, good nightlife - Walking distance to all museums - Mix of local and tourist
Avoid: Immediate Duomo area (too touristy, noisy)

Luxury (€250+/night): - Hotel Lungarno - Arno views, Ferragamo family property - Portrait Firenze - Luxury boutique on Arno - Hotel Savoy - Piazza Repubblica, Rocco Forte

Noise: Request interior courtyard room if light sleeper
Breakfast: Often included, but Florence has great cafes

Luxury (€200+/night): - Romeo Hotel - Modern luxury, waterfront - Grand Hotel Parker’s - Historic, views of bay and Vesuvius - Hotel Excelsior - On Via Partenope, classic

Safety: Choose Chiaia, Vomero, or Lungomare

Noise: Naples is loud - bring earplugs

title: Naples Overview
text:
Napoli - Raw, chaotic, deeply cultured, and home to some of the world’s finest archaeology and the best pizza on Earth. Southern Italy’s largest city is gritty, loud, and utterly authentic - a place that doesn’t try to please tourists but rewards those who embrace its energy.
Our Time Here: 2 full days (June 11-12) + arrival afternoon (June 10)
Why Naples? - Gateway to Herculaneum (better than Pompeii) - World-class archaeological museum (MANN) - Birthplace of pizza - Authentic southern Italian culture - Dramatic setting on the Bay of Naples with Vesuvius backdrop

title: Naples Overview
text:
Napoli - Raw, chaotic, deeply cultured, and home to some of the world’s finest archaeology and the best pizza on Earth. Southern Italy’s largest city is gritty, loud, and utterly authentic - a place that doesn’t try to please tourists but rewards those who embrace its energy.
Our Time Here: 2 full days (June 11-12) + arrival afternoon (June 10)
Why Naples? - Gateway to Herculaneum (better than Pompeii) - World-class archaeological museum (MANN) - Birthplace of pizza - Authentic southern Italian culture - Dramatic setting on the Bay of Naples with Vesuvius backdrop

title: Naples Overview
text:
Trip Context

Arrival (June 10)
High-speed train from Florence (~2.5 hours), arriving early afternoon. Check into hotel, orientation walk, first taste of Neapolitan pizza.


Full Days (June 11-12)
Day 1 (June 11): Herculaneum guided tour (morning/midday), MANN museum or Cappella Sansevero, evening in historic center
Day 2 (June 12): MANN museum (if not done yesterday), Spaccanapoli district, Cappella Sansevero, waterfront walk, final Naples evening


Departure (June 13)
Morning checkout, train to Puglia (Brindisi) - scenic 5-6 hour journey through southern Italy.

title: Naples Overview
text:
Naples at a Glance

The Authentic South
Naples is everything Florence isn’t: chaotic instead of refined, gritty instead of polished, loud instead of reserved. But it’s deeply rewarding:

History: Greek colony (8th century BC), major Roman city, capital of Kingdom of Naples
Culture: Opera (invented here), commedia dell’arte, Neapolitan song
Archaeology: Guarding the treasures of Pompeii and Herculaneum
Food: Pizza Margherita was invented here (1889)
Character: Napoletani are passionate, expressive, and proud

Fair Warning: Naples has rough edges. It’s not dangerous, but it requires awareness and an open mind. The rewards - incredible food, world-class art, genuine Italian life - far outweigh any initial culture shock.

title: Naples Overview
text:
Major Attractions

Archaeological Sites (Our Priority)
Herculaneum ⭐ TOP PRIORITY - Better preserved than Pompeii (sealed by volcanic mud, not ash) - Upper floors, wooden furniture, intact frescoes - The Boat Houses: 300 skeletons visible, most powerful archaeological moment in Italy - 20 minutes by Circumvesuviana train from Naples - Book guided tour in advance (essential for understanding)
Mount Vesuvius (Optional) - The volcano that destroyed Pompeii and Herculaneum (AD 79) - Hike to crater rim (~30 min uphill) - Incredible Bay of Naples views - Can combine with Herculaneum tour - Still active (last eruption 1944)


Museums
MANN - Museo Archeologico Nazionale ⭐ ESSENTIAL - World’s finest collection of Roman artifacts - Most treasures from Pompeii & Herculaneum - Alexander Mosaic (from House of the Faun, Pompeii) - Farnese Collection (massive Roman sculptures) - Secret Cabinet (Roman erotic art) - Egyptian collection - Visit AFTER Herculaneum for context
Cappella Sansevero ⭐ MUST SEE - Giuseppe Sanmartino’s Veiled Christ (1753) - Marble veil appears translucent (one of world’s most astonishing sculptures) - Tiny chapel - book timed entry ahead - 30-45 minutes, unforgettable - No photography allowed


Churches & Historic Sites
Duomo di Napoli (Cathedral) - Chapel of San Gennaro (Naples’ patron saint) - His blood “liquefies” 3 times a year (miraculous) - Baroque architecture
Castel Nuovo (Maschio Angioino) - Medieval castle - Civic Museum - Views of harbor
Napoli Sotterranea (Underground Naples) - 40 meters below street level - Greek aqueducts, Roman theater, WWII shelters - Guided tours only

title: Naples Overview
text:
Neighborhoods

Centro Storico (Historic Center)
Spaccanapoli - Arrow-straight street splitting old Naples - Follows ancient Greek city plan (2,400 years old!) - Churches, shops, street life - Via dei Tribunali parallel street (pizza central)
What to See: - Chiesa del Gesù Nuovo (Baroque church) - Santa Chiara (monastery, beautiful cloister) - San Domenico Maggiore (Gothic church) - Street vendors, nativity workshops, local life
Character: Chaotic, loud, hanging laundry, narrow streets, Vespas everywhere - authentic Naples.


Quartieri Spagnoli (Spanish Quarters)

Grid of narrow streets west of Spaccanapoli
Working-class neighborhood
Maradona murals (he’s a god here)
Excellent cheap food
Not touristy

Safety Note: Fine during day, avoid at night.


Chiaia & Vomero
Chiaia: - Upscale shopping district - Via Chiaia (shopping street) - Safer, cleaner than centro storico - Good hotels and restaurants
Vomero: - Hilltop neighborhood - Residential, quieter - Castel Sant’Elmo (fortress with city views) - Certosa di San Martino (monastery museum) - Funicular from below


Lungomare (Waterfront)

Via Partenope and Via Caracciolo
Promenade along Bay of Naples
Vesuvius views
Castel dell’Ovo (seaside castle)
Excellent seafood restaurants
Cleaner, prettier than centro

Recommended for: Sunset walks, jogging, escaping chaos

title: Naples Overview
text:
Getting Around

Metro
Two Lines: - Line 1 (Orange): Connects Vomero, Centro Storico, station - Line 2 (Blue): Connects Napoli Centrale to Pozzuoli
Tickets: €1.10 for 100 minutes Art Stations: Some stations are museums (Toledo, Università stunning)


Circumvesuviana
Commuter Rail: - Lower level of Napoli Centrale (Garibaldi) station - Goes to: Herculaneum (Ercolano Scavi), Pompeii, Sorrento - €2.80 to Herculaneum - Old trains, watch belongings (pickpockets)


Funicular
Three funiculars to Vomero: - Montesanto, Centrale, Chiaia - €1.10 tickets (same as metro) - Scenic ride up hill


Walking
Centro Storico walkable but: - Chaotic traffic (cars, Vespas, pedestrians all mixed) - Narrow sidewalks or none - Watch for Vespas on sidewalks (yes, really) - Not as pedestrian-friendly as Florence
Pro Tip: Cross streets with confidence (hesitation is dangerous).


Taxis

White cars with “TAXI” sign and meter
Use official taxis only (avoid touts)
Set fare from airport (~€20-25 to center)
Apps: Free Now, it Taxi

Scam Alert: Always insist on meter or agree on price before starting.

title: Naples Overview
text:
Food & Wine

Pizza (The Whole Reason to Visit)
Naples invented pizza. This is where it’s best.
Classic Napoletana: - Thin, soft, charred crust - San Marzano tomatoes - Buffalo mozzarella - Basil - Wood-fired oven (900°F, 90 seconds)
Must-Try Pizzerias:
Da Michele (Since 1870) - Only 2 pizzas: Marinara or Margherita - €5-7, cash only - Expect lines - Pure, simple, perfect
Sorbillo (Via dei Tribunali) - More variety - Always packed - Reservations possible for dinner
50 Kalò (Modern, upscale) - Gourmet pizza - Higher prices (€12-18) - Reservation essential
Rule: If you only eat one meal in Naples, make it pizza.


Other Neapolitan Specialties
Pasta: - Spaghetti alle vongole (with clams) - Pasta alla Genovese (onion ragù, no basil despite name)
Seafood: - Fritto misto (mixed fried seafood) - Polpo (octopus) - Fresh fish (all from Bay of Naples or nearby waters)
Street Food: - Sfogliatella - Shell-shaped pastry (ricotta filling) - Baba al rhum - Rum-soaked sponge cake - Pizza fritta - Fried pizza (filled, folded, deep-fried) - Cuoppo - Fried seafood in paper cone
Coffee: - Espresso culture serious here - €1 at bar (standing) - Never cappuccino after 11 AM


Where to Eat
See: Restaurants page for specific recommendations.
General Tips: - Eat pizza on Via dei Tribunali - Seafood on Lungomare - Cheap local food in Quartieri Spagnoli - Skip tourist traps near port

title: Naples Overview
text:
Practical Information

When We’re There
June 10-12: Early summer - Weather: High 80s-90°F (30-32°C), humid - Crowds: Moderate (fewer tourists than Florence) - Sunset: ~8:30 PM


Safety
Naples has a reputation but it’s largely outdated:
Actual Risks: - Pickpockets: Watch bags in crowded areas, on metro, Circumvesuviana - Bag snatching: Keep purses zipped, crossbody style - Scams: Restaurant touts, overpriced taxis, fake tour guides
NOT a Risk: - Violent crime against tourists (extremely rare) - Wandering during day (perfectly safe)
Be Smart: - Don’t flash expensive jewelry, cameras - Keep valuables close in crowds - Use hotel safe for passports - Stay aware of surroundings - Trust instincts
Neighborhoods to Avoid at Night: - Quartieri Spagnoli (okay during day) - Immediately around Napoli Centrale station - Industrial areas
Safe at Night: - Chiaia - Vomero - Lungomare - Parts of centro storico (stick to main streets)


Money
Budget Per Day (Per Person): - Lodging: €60-100 (shared room) - Food: €25-40 (pizza is cheap!) - Museums/tours: €20-80 (Herculaneum tour is biggest expense) - Transport: €5-10 - Total: €110-230 per day
Cheaper than Florence!


Useful Phrases
Neapolitan Dialect different from standard Italian:

Buongiorno → “Buon’ jorn”
Grazie → “Grazzie” (strong Z)
Come sta? → “Comm’ sta?”

They’ll appreciate any attempt at Italian (or Neapolitan).

title: Naples Overview
text:
Tips for Naples

Embrace the Chaos
Naples is overwhelming at first: - Traffic is insane - Streets are loud - Everything feels disorganized
But that’s the point. Naples doesn’t perform for tourists. It’s authentically itself - take it or leave it. Once you stop expecting Florence’s refinement, you’ll love Naples’ raw energy.


Don’t Compare to Florence
Different cities, different vibes: - Florence: Museum city, refined, Renaissance - Naples: Living city, gritty, authentic
Both are incredible. Judge Naples on its own terms.


The Herculaneum Decision
Most tourists go to Pompeii. We’re going to Herculaneum because: - Better preserved (organic materials intact) - Smaller, more manageable (not overwhelming) - Fewer crowds - More emotionally powerful (the Boat Houses) - Closer to Naples (20 min vs. 40 min)
If you have time for only one: Herculaneum > Pompeii.


MANN Museum Timing
Go AFTER Herculaneum: - Seeing the ruins first gives context - Artifacts make more sense - You’ll appreciate what you’re looking at
Not before - it’s backwards chronologically.

title: Naples Overview
section: Our Naples Itinerary at a Glance
text:
Our Naples Itinerary at a Glance
Day 1 (June 11): - Morning/midday: Herculaneum guided tour - Afternoon: MANN museum OR Cappella Sansevero (depending on energy) - Evening: Pizza on Via dei Tribunali, Spaccanapoli walk
Day 2 (June 12): - Morning: MANN museum (if not done) OR Cappella Sansevero - Midday: Spaccanapoli district exploration - Afternoon: Lungomare waterfront walk, Castel dell’Ovo - Evening: Farewell dinner (seafood or pizza), gelato, packing for Puglia
See Daily Itineraries in sidebar for detailed schedules.

title: Day 16: Departure from Brindisi
text:
Morning: Brindisi Airport (8:00–10:55am)

Getting to the Airport
Brindisi Airport (BDS) — Papola Casale Airport - Located ~4 km from Brindisi center - Short taxi ride from hotel (~10–15 minutes, €10–15) - Allow 2.5 hours before departure for international check-in
Depart hotel by: 8:15am at the latest


Check-In

Check in online 24 hours before (Lufthansa app or lufthansa.com)
Have passport ready
Check baggage allowance: 2x23kg checked + 1x8kg carry-on per person
Seats will be assigned at check-in (assigned seating requires check-in)



Brindisi Airport Notes

Small regional airport — no large terminal mall
One departure hall, straightforward security
Limited food/coffee options airside — eat breakfast at hotel before leaving
Duty-free is minimal

title: Day 16: Departure from Brindisi
text:
Flight 1: Brindisi → Munich (LH 1895)
Operated by: Air Dolomiti (regional Lufthansa partner) Aircraft: Embraer 195 Jet Duration: 1 hour 50 minutes Cabin: Economy Meals: Refreshment / Food & Beverage for Purchase

Departs BDS 10:55am → Arrives MUC 12:45pm (Terminal 2)
Compact regional jet — overhead bins fill fast, board early
No seat assignment until check-in
This short hop gets you into Lufthansa’s main Munich hub

title: Day 16: Departure from Brindisi
text:
Munich Layover (12:45pm–3:40pm) — ~3 Hours
Munich Airport (MUC) — Terminal 2
Stay in Terminal 2 — both MUC flights use T2 (Lufthansa hub). No terminal changes needed.
Layover checklist: - [ ] Clear EU passport control (if not already cleared in BDS — likely just a transit) - [ ] Confirm UA 8855 departure gate on boards - [ ] Have lunch at Munich airport — excellent options in T2 (Bavarian food, good coffee) - [ ] Browse duty-free if desired - [ ] Find departure gate before 3:15pm boarding call
Time to kill: ~3 hours is comfortable. Don’t leave the secured area.

title: Day 16: Departure from Brindisi
text:
Flight 2: Munich → Chicago (UA 8855)
Operated by: Lufthansa for United Aircraft: Airbus A359 (widebody long-haul) Duration: 9 hours 35 minutes Cabin: Premium Economy Meals: Full meal service included

Departs MUC 3:40pm (T2) → Arrives ORD 6:15pm (International Terminal 5)
Premium Economy: wider seats, more legroom, dedicated cabin
9.5-hour transatlantic crossing — longest leg of the day
Tips: sleep if possible, stay hydrated, walk the aisle every 2 hours

Arriving Chicago: US Customs and Immigration at Terminal 5 (international arrivals) - Fill out/confirm customs declaration - Collect checked bags at T5 baggage claim - Clear customs (“Nothing to Declare” if applicable) - Recheck bags for domestic connection — bags must be rechecked after customs

title: Day 16: Departure from Brindisi
text:
Chicago O’Hare Domestic Connection (6:15pm–9:15pm) — ~3 Hours
After clearing customs at Terminal 5:

Collect bags, clear customs
Recheck bags at United check-in for MSN flight (domestic counter, Terminal 1)
Take the ATS train to Terminal 1 (United domestic)
Re-clear security (TSA)
Find gate for UA 1400 to Madison

Time: 3-hour connection is comfortable for international-to-domestic at ORD. Don’t panic — customs can be 30–45 minutes, but you have margin.
Food: ORD Terminal 1 has many dining options. Grab dinner before the final hop.

title: Day 16: Departure from Brindisi
text:
Flight 3: Chicago → Madison (UA 1400)
Aircraft: Boeing 737-700 Duration: 1 hour 12 minutes Cabin: Premium Economy Seats: 08D (Dan) / 08E (Jane) Meals: No meal service

Departs ORD 9:15pm (Terminal 1) → Arrives MSN 10:27pm
Short final leg home
You’re almost there!

title: Day 16: Departure from Brindisi
text:
Arrival: Madison (10:27pm)
Welcome home. After 16 days, 3 Italian cities, and countless memories, you land in Madison just after 10:30pm.
After landing: - Short walk to baggage claim at Dane County Regional Airport - Ground transportation: taxi, rideshare, or pre-arranged pickup - Don’t drive if you’re exhausted — consider a taxi/Uber home
Jet lag note: You’ve been in Italy (UTC+2 in June). Madison is UTC-5/CDT. That’s a 7-hour difference. Expect to feel it for 2–3 days.

title: Day 16: Departure from Brindisi
text:
Packing Up for Departure

The Night Before (June 19)

Repack all luggage — make sure nothing exceeds 2x23kg checked / 1x8kg carry-on
Set aside passport, boarding passes, and travel documents in carry-on
Charge all devices overnight
Settle hotel bill, confirm checkout time
Book/confirm taxi to BDS airport for 8:15am

title: Day 10: First Full Day in Puglia
section: Morning: Brindisi on Foot (9 AM–1 PM)
text:
Morning: Brindisi on Foot (9 AM–1 PM)
After the intensity of Florence and Naples, Brindisi is refreshingly calm. A working Mediterranean port, not a tourist hotspot — which is exactly the point.

The Waterfront & Historic Center
Start: Coffee at a local bar near the hotel (not a tourist café — ask the hotel where the locals go).
Walk the inner harbor (Seno di Ponente):







Site
What It Is




Colonne Romane
One intact Roman column marking the end of the Appian Way (Via Appia). This is where ancient Rome physically ended — Julius Caesar, Cicero, Virgil all passed through.


Seno di Ponente
The sheltered inner harbor — fishing boats, Mediterranean ferries, unhurried pace


Castello Svevo
Norman-era castle on the harbor; exterior worth seeing


Piazza Vittorio Emanuele
Main square near the waterfront


Corso Garibaldi
Pedestrian main street into centro storico



Walk time: 60–90 minutes, relaxed pace.


Museo Archeologico Nazionale di Brindisi (Optional)

Modest museum with finds from the ancient Greek and Roman city
Free or low cost entry; helpful context for the columns and the port’s 2,500-year history
Located near the waterfront
crumbs: Day 10: First Full Day in Puglia

title: Day 10: First Full Day in Puglia
text:
Afternoon: Rest & Puglia Context (1–5 PM)
This is a recovery afternoon. You’ve spent a week in Florence and nearly four days in Naples. Puglia is beautiful but it’s also six more days of exploration. Spend this afternoon reading, resting, or strolling lightly.
Use the time for: - [ ] Read Puglia Overview to understand what you’ll be visiting - [ ] Review tour details and confirm pickup times/meeting points - [ ] Check accommodations for the coming days - [ ] Get cash if needed — ATMs are easy to find in centro - [ ] A leisurely lunch near the harbor
Light option: Walk to the Tempio di San Giovanni al Sepolcro (a small round Crusader church from the 11th century — easy to miss, worth finding).
crumbs: Day 10: First Full Day in Puglia

title: Day 10: First Full Day in Puglia
text:
Evening: Dinner in Puglia (7–9 PM)
You started getting acquainted with Puglian food last night. Tonight, go deeper.
Puglia’s table:







Dish
What to Know




Orecchiette alle cime di rapa
The definitive Puglian pasta — “little ears” with bitter turnip greens and anchovy. Get it if it’s on the menu.


Burrata
If you haven’t had it fresh in Puglia yet, this is the night. Born in the region — nothing like what you get abroad.


Fave e cicorie
Fava bean purée with bitter greens — ancient peasant dish, stunning


Tiella di riso, patate e cozze
Layered bake of rice, potatoes, and mussels — a Bari/Puglia classic


Primitivo
Fuller-bodied red from Manduria — don’t skip it



Seafood: Brindisi is a fishing port. Whatever the restaurant says is fresh today, get that.
Where to look: - Side streets off Corso Garibaldi heading into centro storico - Avoid restaurants with multilingual photo menus on the waterfront - Ask the hotel for a specific recommendation — they know

title: Day 8: Naples — Museums & City
text: Second full day in Naples: MANN museum (if not done yesterday), Cappella Sansevero, and an afternoon in the historic center. This is the day for the things that don’t require a day trip.

title: Day 8: Naples — Museums & City
text:
Morning: Museums (9 AM–1 PM)
Priority 1: MANN — Museo Archeologico Nazionale (if not done yesterday)
See: Museums - MANN

Allow 2–3 hours
Highlights: treasures from Pompeii & Herculaneum, Farnese Collection, Alexander Mosaic, Secret Cabinet
Now that you’ve been to Herculaneum, the objects have context — this visit lands differently
€18 entry; closed Tuesdays (not an issue — today is Friday); metro to Museo stop

Priority 2: Cappella Sansevero
See: Museums - Cappella Sansevero

Book timed entry in advance — this tiny chapel has very limited slots and sells out
The Veiled Christ by Giuseppe Sanmartino (1753) is one of the most extraordinary sculptures in existence — a marble veil that appears genuinely transparent
30–45 minutes; €9 entry; no photography (enforced)
Located in the historic center near Piazza San Domenico Maggiore

Do Both if Possible: - MANN 9am–12pm - Cappella Sansevero 12:30–1:30pm (a short walk away)

title: Day 8: Naples — Museums & City
text:
Afternoon: Spaccanapoli & Historic Center (2–6 PM)
Spaccanapoli: - The ancient straight street that cuts across historic Naples, following a 2,400-year-old Greek city grid - More concentrated chaos and authenticity than anywhere else in the south
Must-See Along the Way: - Gesù Nuovo — Baroque church with an unusual diamond-cut stone facade - Santa Chiara — Monastery with a beautiful cloister covered in majolica tiles - San Domenico Maggiore — Gothic church; the one Aquinas preached in - Piazza Bellini — Ruins of the Greek city walls exposed below the piazza floor
Experience: - Street food: cuoppo (cone of fried things), pizza fritta, taralli - Presepi (nativity) workshops — Naples is famous for these year-round - The laundry, the scooters, the noise — this is what Naples actually is
Via dei Tribunali: - Parallel street to Spaccanapoli, slightly wider - More pizza options if you can still eat pizza - Underground Naples tours start here

title: Day 8: Naples — Museums & City
text:
Late Afternoon: Waterfront (5–7 PM)
Lungomare Promenade: - Via Partenope and Via Caracciolo hug the bay - Vesuvius views across the water - Castel dell’Ovo (seaside castle) on the small island - Calmer and airier than the historic center — a welcome contrast
Activities: - Sunset walk along the water - Photos of Vesuvius (dramatic in the late-afternoon light) - Gelato on the promenade

title: Day 8: Naples — Museums & City
text:
Evening: Farewell Naples (7–10 PM)
Final Dinner: - Return to favorite pizzeria? - Or seafood at a Lungomare restaurant - Quartieri Spagnoli (Spanish Quarter) has excellent trattorie and a gritty local feel
After Dinner: - Final sfogliatella or pastiera (traditional Naples cake) - Evening stroll - Pack tonight for Saturday’s departure

Suggested Final Walk

This is Italo’s premium Lounge class — leather seats, a quieter car, and included service. It’s not a budget economy seat; sit back and enjoy the ride.
Journey highlights: - Tuscany countryside rolls past first — vineyards, cypress trees, terra-cotta farmhouses - The train plunges through Apennine tunnels mid-route — watch for the dramatic switchover from green Tuscany to the sun-baked south - Sit on the right side approaching Naples for possible Vesuvius views across the plain
Duration: 3 hours even (12:28 → 15:28)

title: Day 6: Florence to Naples
text:
Arrive Naples (3:28pm)

Napoli Centrale Station
You’ll emerge from a sleek high-speed train into one of Italy’s most chaotic stations. This is normal — Naples is different.
After arriving:

Follow “Uscita” (exit) signs toward Piazza Garibaldi
Watch your belongings — the station and piazza are busy pickpocket zones; keep bags zipped and phones in pockets
Get to the taxi stand immediately outside and take an official white taxi — agree on price or insist on the meter (~€15–20 to most central hotels)
Avoid anyone offering “taxi” on the concourse — use only the official stand



Hotel Check-In (~4:00–4:30pm)

Drop bags, freshen up, breathe
Ask for restaurant recommendations and a neighborhood map
Get the WiFi password
Note: Naples is louder and more chaotic than Florence. That’s the charm — lean into it.

title: Day 6: Florence to Naples
section: Evening: First Night in Naples (7–10 PM)
text:
Evening: First Night in Naples (7–10 PM)
Pizza is the only correct choice for tonight.
Naples invented pizza. Specifically, the Margherita was created here in 1889. Tonight you honor that.
See: Restaurants — Naples Pizza
Top options:

Da Michele — Legendary, cash only, expect a line, worth it. Only two pizzas: Margherita or Marinara.
Sorbillo — Via dei Tribunali, excellent, often crowded
50 Kalò — More upscale, worth reserving ahead

What to order: - Margherita (tomato, mozzarella, basil) — the original, invented here - Marinara (tomato, garlic, oregano, no cheese) — older than the Margherita, equally essential - Cost: €5–12 depending on place
After pizza: - Sfogliatella — shell-shaped ricotta pastry, a Naples specialty — grab one from a pasticceria - Short evening walk toward Spaccanapoli or the waterfront - Early to bed: Herculaneum is tomorrow

Morning: Accademia Gallery (8:30-10 AM)

Galleria dell’Accademia
Booking: Check timed entry - arrive 15 min early
Highlights: - David ⭐ - The icon, 17 feet tall, perfection in marble - Stand directly in front, walk around, take your time - Unfinished Prisoners showing Michelangelo’s process - Musical instrument collection
Time: 1-1.5 hours (smaller than Uffizi)
Tips: - Gets crowded by 10 AM - early entry best - Photography allowed (no flash) - Everyone here for David - don’t miss the Prisoners too

Medici family church, next to chapels - Brunelleschi architecture - Laurentian Library (Michelangelo staircase) - if open

title: Day 2: Frankfurt to Florence
text:
Arrival in Frankfurt (2:05pm)

Landing at Frankfurt Airport (FRA) Terminal 1
After 8.5 hours in the air, you arrive in Frankfurt — one of Europe’s largest transit hubs. No need to exit the airport or clear full customs (you’re in transit to Florence).
After deplaning: 1. Follow signs for Connections / Anschlussflüge (do not follow signs for “Arrivals/Ausgang”) 2. If needed, clear Schengen entry passport control (for transit within EU, often not required — follow agent instructions) 3. Check departure boards for LH 9498 gate 4. Terminal 1 is large — allow 30–45 minutes to reach your gate


Frankfurt Layover (2:05pm–4:50pm) — ~2 Hours 45 Minutes
This is a comfortable connection — but Frankfurt is large. Stay efficient.

Find LH 9498 departure gate on boards
Get coffee and a snack — there are excellent options throughout T1
Use the bathroom and freshen up
Be at gate by 4:20pm (30 min before departure)

If you feel fresh enough: Terminal 1 has a small duty-free area and shops — good place to pick up a Frankfurt airport magnet.

title: Day 2: Frankfurt to Florence
text:
Flight 3: Frankfurt → Florence (LH 9498)
Operated by: Air Dolomiti (Lufthansa regional partner) Aircraft: Embraer 195 Jet (regional — compact, efficient) Duration: 1 hour 40 minutes Cabin: Economy Meals: Food & Beverage for Purchase (short flight)

Departs FRA Terminal 1, 4:50pm
Arrives FLR (Florence Peretola Airport), 6:30pm


About the Embraer 195

2-2 seating configuration (no middle seat!)
Overhead bins smaller than widebody jets — carry-on should fit, but board early
Stunning Alpine views on approach to Italy (sit on right side of plane for potential Alps glimpse)

title: Day 2: Frankfurt to Florence
text:
Arrival: Florence (6:30pm)

Florence Airport — Amerigo Vespucci Airport (FLR)
Florence’s airport is small and easy. You’ll be out quickly.
After landing: 1. Passport/EU entry control — Have passport ready. If you didn’t clear Schengen at Frankfurt, you’ll clear it here. 2. Baggage claim — Collect both checked bags. Small airport = bags arrive quickly (~15–20 min) 3. Customs — Walk through “Nothing to Declare” (green lane) 4. Arrivals hall — You’re in Italy!


Getting to Your Hotel (~7:00–7:30pm)
Option A: Taxi (Recommended) - Official taxi stand immediately outside arrivals - Fixed fare to city center: €22–25 - ~20 minutes to most Florence hotels - No app needed — just queue at the white taxis
Option B: Tram (T2 Line) - Florence’s new airport tram (T2) connects FLR to the city center - Cost: ~€1.50, every 8–10 minutes - ~25 minutes to SMN (Santa Maria Novella station area) - Good option if hotel is near the historic center and you have manageable luggage


Hotel Check-In (~7:00–8:00pm)

Most hotels hold reservations until midnight — no rush
Ask for luggage storage if checking in early in future
Get a city map from the front desk
Ask about breakfast times and hours

title: Day 2: Frankfurt to Florence
text:
Evening: First Night in Florence

Managing Arrival Energy
You’ve been traveling for roughly 24 hours (since leaving home yesterday). You’ll be tired but probably buzzing with excitement.
The right approach: - Don’t try to see the Uffizi tonight - A short walk to orient yourself is perfect - Find a restaurant within walking distance of the hotel - Eat a proper meal and drink water - Be in bed by 10pm Italian time to start resetting your clock


First Florence Walk (30–45 minutes)
After dropping bags and freshening up, take a short evening walk: - Find the Arno River — just follow it, you’ll know you’re in Florence - Cross one of the bridges if you’re close (Ponte Vecchio is the famous one) - Get gelato (your first in Italy — important) - Just soak in being here


Dinner

Pick something close to the hotel — tonight is not the night for a destination restaurant
Look for a simple trattoria with pasta and house wine
Avoid the tourist traps immediately adjacent to major sights (overpriced, worse food)
Ask the hotel front desk for a nearby recommendation

title: Day 1: Madison to Frankfurt
text:
Morning & Afternoon: Final Preparations

Before You Leave Home

Confirm flight status: lufthansa.com or the Lufthansa app
Check in online opens 23 hours before: check in for LH 8835 (MSN→ORD) and LH 0433 (ORD→FRA) separately
Charge all electronics: phone, camera, portable battery pack
Pack carry-on with 24-hour essentials (clothes, medications, toiletries)
Have passport in carry-on — not checked bag
Verify credit cards: notify banks of travel dates
Empty refrigerator of perishables
Set thermostat, lock windows and doors, take out trash
Confirm pet/plant care arranged



Luggage Allowance
Both checked and carry-on bags: - Checked: 2x23kg (50 lbs) per person - Carry-on: 1 piece per person (no weight stated for LH 8835 leg)
What to put in carry-on: - Passport (non-negotiable) - Boarding passes (digital + printed backup) - Medications (full supply — never checked) - 1–2 changes of clothes (in case bag is delayed) - Phone charger + portable battery - Snacks - Eye mask, earplugs, neck pillow (for overnight flight) - Entertainment: downloaded shows, books, podcasts

title: Day 1: Madison to Frankfurt
text:
Flight 1: Madison → Chicago (LH 8835)
Operated by: GoJet Airlines for United Express for United Airlines Aircraft: Air 109 Duration: 1 hour 15 minutes Cabin: Economy | Seats: 15C (Dan), 15D (Jane) Meals: No meal service

Departs MSN 5:45pm → Arrives ORD 7:00pm (Terminal 2)
Short regional hop — arrive at airport by 3:45pm (2 hours early for international connection)
After landing at ORD Terminal 2, make your way to Terminal 1 for the Lufthansa departure

title: Day 1: Madison to Frankfurt
text:
Chicago O’Hare Layover (7:00pm–10:30pm) — ~3.5 Hours
At O’Hare: - Arrive Terminal 2 (United) - Take the ATS people mover to Terminal 1 (Lufthansa international departures) - Follow signs for LH 0433 gate - Allow 30–45 minutes to get between terminals and through any additional security
Use the time: - Eat dinner — 10.5+ hours until landing in Frankfurt - Find the gate and confirm it on departure boards - Fill water bottle for the flight - Visit airport lounge if you have access (Amex Platinum, Chase Sapphire, etc.) - Stretch and walk — long flight ahead

title: Day 1: Madison to Frankfurt
text:
Flight 2: Chicago → Frankfurt (LH 0433)
Aircraft: Airbus A343 (wide-body long-haul jet) Duration: 8 hours 35 minutes Cabin: Premium Economy — wider seats, more legroom, dedicated service Meals: Refreshments + full meal service

Departs ORD Terminal 1, 10:30pm (Friday, June 5)
Arrives FRA Terminal 1, 2:05pm (Saturday, June 6)


Premium Economy on Lufthansa A343
What to expect: - Dedicated Premium Economy cabin (rows 20–30 typically) - Seat width: ~18–19 inches, seat pitch: ~38 inches - Adjustable footrest, headrest, lumbar support - Premium meal service with real crockery - Dedicated overhead bin space - Priority boarding after Business class


Sleep Strategy (Transatlantic)
Florence is UTC+2 in June (7 hours ahead of Madison CDT). To minimize jet lag: - Set your watch to Italian time immediately at boarding (~17:30 Italian time when you board) - Flight arrives Frankfurt at 2:05pm Italian time — try to sleep 7+ hours on board - Decline meals if they interfere with sleep - Use eye mask and earplugs - Recline seat fully, use footrest


In-Flight Tips
Do: - Hydrate steadily (water, avoid excessive alcohol) - Walk aisles every 2 hours (compression socks help) - Sleep on an Italian schedule
Don’t: - Overdrink alcohol (dehydrates, worsens jet lag) - Stay seated the entire flight

Breakfast (7:30-8 AM)
Café Breakfast: - Find local café near hotel - Order: “Un cappuccino e un cornetto, per favore” - Stand at bar (cheaper) or sit (more expensive but relaxing) - Cost: €3-5 - Watch Florentines start their day


Duomo Complex
See: Museums Research - Duomo
What’s Included in Combo Ticket (€30): - Cathedral nave (free, but combo ticket needed for other sites) - Brunelleschi’s Dome climb ⭐ MUST DO - Giotto’s Bell Tower (Campanile) - Baptistery - Opera Museum - Crypt
Priority Order:
1. Dome Climb (463 steps) - FIRST - Timed entry required - check your booking time - Go early before heat of day - No elevator, narrow passages (not for claustrophobic) - Incredible views of Florence from top - See Vasari’s Last Judgment frescoes up close - Allow 45-60 minutes
2. Cathedral Interior - Free entry to nave - Massive scale, relatively simple interior - Look up at dome from inside - 20-30 minutes
3. Baptistery - Ghiberti’s “Gates of Paradise” bronze doors - Byzantine mosaics in dome - 20 minutes
4. Bell Tower (Optional) - 414 steps, different views than dome - Skip if tired from dome climb

title: Day 3: Florence - Duomo & Uffizi
text:

Uffizi Gallery ⭐ PRIORITY
See: Museums Research - Uffizi
Booking: Check your timed entry - arrive 15 min early
Strategy: - This is HUGE - pace yourself - Focus on highlights, don’t try to see everything - Take breaks on benches
Must-See Rooms:
Room 10-14: Botticelli - Birth of Venus - Primavera - The reason you’re here
Room 15: Leonardo da Vinci - Annunciation - Adoration of the Magi
Room 35: Michelangelo - Doni Tondo (Holy Family)
Room 90: Caravaggio - Medusa - Bacchus
Also Great: - Raphael, Titian, Giotto, Duccio - Rooftop café (Duomo views)
Time Needed: 2.5-4 hours (depends on stamina)

title: Day 7: Naples — Herculaneum
text: First full day in Naples. Today’s highlight is Herculaneum — an ancient Roman city buried in volcanic mud by Vesuvius in 79 AD, better preserved than Pompeii and more emotionally powerful. A licensed guide makes the difference here.

title: Day 7: Naples — Herculaneum
text:
Morning: Herculaneum Tour (Depart ~8–9 AM)

Tour Details
See: Tours Research - Herculaneum
Meeting: - Check tour confirmation for exact time and meeting point - Likely hotel pickup OR meet at Ercolano Scavi train station - Bring: comfortable shoes, water, sun protection, camera
Getting There (if independent): - Circumvesuviana train from Napoli Porta Nolana or Centrale to Ercolano Scavi (~20 min) - €2.80 one-way; runs every 20–30 minutes - The station literally overlooks the excavated city
At Herculaneum (~4–5 hours total):

Licensed archaeologist guide (worth every euro — the context transforms the experience)
2-hour guided tour of ruins

What You’ll See:







Site
Why It Matters




The Boat Houses
300 skeletons of people who fled to the shore — most powerful moment on the site


House of Neptune & Amphitrite
Vivid blue-and-gold mosaics, astonishingly preserved


Casa dei Cervi
Elite villa with original frescoes and garden statues


Thermopolium
Ancient fast-food counter with food residue still identifiable


Wooden structures
Actual original wood — doors, furniture, beams — impossible at Pompeii



Why Better than Pompeii: - Upper floors of buildings are intact (Pompeii’s collapsed) - Organic materials preserved — wood, cloth, bread, wine - Far less crowded - More manageable size (2 hours vs. a full day) - More emotionally powerful: the boat houses are unlike anything else in Italy

title: Day 7: Naples — Herculaneum
text:
Afternoon Options (2–6 PM)
Option A: MANN Museum (Recommended if energy remains)
See: Museums - MANN

The National Archaeological Museum holds the treasures removed from Herculaneum and Pompeii
Go AFTER Herculaneum — the context transforms the objects completely
Highlights: Farnese Collection, Alexander Mosaic, Secret Cabinet (erotica from Pompeii)
Allow 2–3 hours; €18 entry; closed Tuesdays
Located in the Museo/Cavour area, easy taxi or metro from Ercolano

Option B: Cappella Sansevero
See: Museums - Cappella Sansevero

The Veiled Christ — marble veil so lifelike people have refused to believe it’s stone
Book timed entry in advance — tiny chapel, very limited slots
30–45 minutes; €9 entry; no photography (seriously enforced)
In the historic center, easy to combine with an evening walk

Option C: Rest
Herculaneum is intense — emotionally and physically. Coming back to the hotel, resting, and saving the museums for tomorrow is completely valid.

text:
Evening: First Night in Puglia (7:30–10 PM)
Welcome to the heel of the boot. The cuisine changes immediately — you’re no longer in Naples.
Puglia staples that start tonight:







Dish
Description




Orecchiette
“Little ears” pasta, often with turnip greens (cime di rapa) or ragù


Burrata
Fresh mozzarella filled with cream — Puglia’s most famous gift


Fave e cicorie
Fava bean purée with bitter greens — ancient, extraordinary


Primitivo
Local red wine — rich and full-bodied, from Manduria


Negroamaro
Another Puglian red — darker, more tannic



Where to eat: Walk toward the inner harbor and look for family-run trattorie on Corso Garibaldi or the streets into centro storico. Avoid anything immediately adjacent to the tourist waterfront.

Optional: Walk the Inner Harbor
Brindisi’s waterfront is compact and walkable — even after a travel day:

Colonne Romane — Two Roman columns (one intact) marking the end of the Appian Way; where ancient Rome ended. Julius Caesar, Cicero, Augustus walked here.
Seno di Ponente — The sheltered inner harbor; fishing boats and Mediterranean ferries
Castello Svevo — Norman-era castle overlooking the harbor







NoteEasy Does It Tonight



You’ve done two flights and spent a week each in Florence and Naples. Tonight is dinner, a short walk, and sleep. Puglia starts tomorrow.

title: Italy Trip 2026
text:
Why Italy? Why These Regions?
Italy offers layers of history that few places can match. Florence gave birth to the Renaissance, preserving centuries of artistic genius in its museums and churches. Naples guards some of the world’s finest archaeological treasures from Pompeii and Herculaneum. And Puglia—Italy’s hidden gem—beckons with its unique trulli houses, baroque architecture, and authentic southern Italian culture largely untouched by mass tourism.
This trip combines three distinct Italian experiences: - Florence: Four days immersed in Renaissance art—Michelangelo’s David, Botticelli’s Birth of Venus, Brunelleschi’s Dome, and countless treasures from humanity’s creative peak - Naples: Two days exploring the archaeological wonders of Herculaneum and the vibrant, chaotic energy of southern Italy’s largest city - Puglia: Six days discovering the lesser-known heel of Italy’s boot, with its whitewashed hill towns, coastal beauty, and distinctive regional character

title: Italy Trip 2026
text:
Trip Overview
June 5-6 (Days 1-2): Departure & Arrival Fly Madison → Chicago → Frankfurt overnight (Premium Economy), arriving Florence June 6 at 6:30pm via Air Dolomiti
June 6-9 (Days 3-6): Florence Four full days exploring the Uffizi Gallery, Accademia, Duomo Complex, Bargello, Medici Chapels, Palazzo Pitti, Santa Croce, and the art-filled streets of the Renaissance capital
June 10-12 (Days 7-9): Naples & Herculaneum High-speed train to Naples, guided tour of the exceptionally preserved ruins of Herculaneum, exploration of Naples’ archaeological museum (MANN) and historic center
June 13 (Day 10): Journey to Puglia Scenic train ride through the Apennines and down the Adriatic coast, entering Puglia and arriving in Brindisi
June 14-19 (Days 11-16): Puglia Six-day tour exploring Puglia’s highlights—Alberobello’s UNESCO trulli houses, Lecce’s baroque splendor, Polignano a Mare’s dramatic coastline, Ostuni’s white city, regional cuisine and wine
June 20 (Day 16): Departure from Brindisi Flight home from Brindisi (BDS → MUC → ORD → MSN) to Madison

title: Italy Trip 2026
text:
Our Approach
This journey balances structure and spontaneity. The Florence and Naples portions are carefully planned around museum reservations and train schedules, while still leaving time to wander, discover, and embrace the Italian pace of life. The Puglia tour will provide expert guidance through a less-familiar region. Throughout, we’re prioritizing:

Art and archaeology: From Renaissance masterpieces to Roman ruins
Authentic experiences: Local food, markets, neighborhoods beyond tourist zones
Flexibility: Room for unexpected discoveries and recommendations from locals
Documentation: Photos, notes, and reflections to remember and share

This website is both planning tool and travel journal—a living document that evolves from research to reality as we prepare for and experience Italy.

Join us as we trace the arc of Italian history from Renaissance Florence through ancient Herculaneum to the sun-soaked landscapes of Puglia. Andiamo! 🇮🇹

title: Florence Overview

Trip Context

Arrival (June 5)
Arriving from Pisa by train (~1 hour), checking into hotel, afternoon orientation walk. This is our soft landing after overnight flight - gentle exploration, early dinner, rest.


Full Days (June 6-9)
Four complete days dedicated to Florence’s artistic and cultural treasures:
Day 1 (June 6): Duomo Complex, Uffizi Gallery Day 2 (June 7): Accademia (David), Medici sites Day 3 (June 8): Bargello, Santa Croce, markets Day 4 (June 9): Palazzo Pitti, Boboli Gardens, final museums, shopping


Departure (June 10)
Morning checkout, high-speed train to Naples.

title: Florence Overview
text:
Florence at a Glance

The Renaissance Happened Here
1400-1600: Florence exploded with artistic and intellectual genius, producing: - Michelangelo, Leonardo da Vinci, Botticelli, Raphael (artists) - Dante, Machiavelli, Galileo (writers, thinkers) - The Medici family (patrons who funded it all) - Brunelleschi’s architectural innovations - Linear perspective, humanist philosophy, modern science
Result: The Renaissance spread from Florence to transform Europe and, eventually, the world.


The Medici Family
Why They Matter: For 300 years (1434-1737), this banking family ruled Florence and bankrolled the Renaissance.
What They Left: - Funded construction of the Duomo’s dome - Commissioned countless masterpieces - Built Palazzo Pitti, Palazzo Vecchio - Established the Uffizi Gallery - Patronized Michelangelo, Leonardo, Botticelli - Founded the Medici Chapels (their mausoleum)
Without the Medici: No Sistine Chapel ceiling (they commissioned Michelangelo), no Birth of Venus, no David statue.

title: Florence Overview
text:
Major Attractions

Art Museums (Priority Bookings Required)
Uffizi Gallery - World’s finest Renaissance art collection - Botticelli’s Birth of Venus and Primavera - Leonardo, Michelangelo, Raphael, Caravaggio - Book 2-3 months ahead
Galleria dell’Accademia - Michelangelo’s David - Unfinished Prisoners sculptures - Book 2-3 months ahead
Bargello Museum - Best sculpture collection - Donatello’s bronze David - Less crowded than Uffizi
Palazzo Pitti - Palatine Gallery (Raphael, Titian) - Royal Apartments - Boboli Gardens


Churches & Architecture
Duomo Complex - Cathedral of Santa Maria del Fiore - Brunelleschi’s Dome (463-step climb, book ahead!) - Giotto’s Bell Tower (Campanile) - Baptistery with Ghiberti’s “Gates of Paradise”
Santa Croce - Franciscan church - Tombs of Michelangelo, Galileo, Machiavelli - Giotto frescoes
Medici Chapels - Michelangelo’s sculptural tombs - Opulent Chapel of Princes
San Lorenzo - Medici family church - Laurentian Library (Michelangelo staircase)
San Marco - Fra Angelico frescoes - Former monastery


Iconic Sites
Ponte Vecchio - Medieval bridge with jewelry shops - Only bridge Nazis didn’t destroy in WWII - Sunset views of Arno River
Piazza della Signoria - Florence’s civic square since Middle Ages - Palazzo Vecchio (city hall) - Copy of David (original was here) - Loggia dei Lanzi (outdoor sculpture gallery)
Piazzale Michelangelo - Hilltop overlook with panoramic Florence views - Sunset essential - Copy of David statue

title: Florence Overview
text:
Neighborhoods

Centro Storico (Historic Center)
Duomo Area - Tourist epicenter - Cathedral, baptistery, museums - Crowded but must-see - Avoid staying here (too touristy, noisy)
Santa Maria Novella (Near train station) - Convenient for arrivals/departures - Good hotels and restaurants - Church of Santa Maria Novella (beautiful facade) - Pharmacy of Santa Maria Novella (oldest in Europe, 1612)


San Lorenzo / Mercato Centrale

Local character, less touristy
Mercato Centrale (food market - amazing!)
Leather market stalls
Near Medici Chapels and San Lorenzo church
Great place to stay



Oltrarno (Across the River)

“Other side of the Arno”
Artisan workshops, antique shops
Palazzo Pitti and Boboli Gardens
Excellent local restaurants
Quieter at night
Santo Spirito square (evening aperitivo scene)



Santa Croce

Vibrant piazza with nightlife
Santa Croce church
Mix of local and tourist
Walking distance to everything

title: Florence Overview
text:
Getting Around

Walking City
Florence is MADE for walking: - Historic center is compact (1 mile across) - Most sights within 20-minute walk - Pedestrian zones (no cars)
Expect: - 5-10 miles of walking per day - Cobblestone streets (wear good shoes!) - Sore feet (totally worth it)


Public Transportation
Buses: - ATAF orange buses - €1.50 for 90 minutes - Buy tickets at tabacchi (tobacco) shops or machines - Validate on bus (yellow machines)
Not Really Needed: - Historic center is walkable - Exception: Piazzale Michelangelo (#12 or #13 bus)


Taxis

White cars with “TAXI” sign
Expensive by U.S. standards (€10-15 for short rides)
Use for: airport, late night, heavy bags
App: it Taxi or Free Now

title: Florence Overview
text:
Food & Wine

Tuscan Specialties
Must Try: - Bistecca alla Fiorentina - Massive T-bone steak, rare, shared - Ribollita - Hearty bread and bean soup - Pappardelle al cinghiale - Wide pasta with wild boar ragù - Lampredotto - Tripe sandwich (street food) - Schiacciata - Tuscan flatbread - Cantucci e Vin Santo - Almond cookies dunked in sweet wine


Wine
Tuscany Wines: - Chianti Classico (red, nearby) - Brunello di Montalcino (red, expensive, worth it) - Vino Nobile di Montepulciano (red) - Vernaccia di San Gimignano (white)
House Wine (Vino della Casa): Often excellent and cheap


Gelato
Best Gelato: - Vivoli - Gelateria dei Neri - La Carraia
Avoid: Neon colors, piled-high displays (tourist traps)


Markets
Mercato Centrale (Upstairs food court, downstairs produce) - Breakfast, lunch, snacks - Excellent prepared foods - Wine bar, cheese, pasta, pizza, truffles
Mercato di Sant’Ambrogio - Local market, less touristy - Fresh produce, meat, cheese

title: Florence Overview
text:
Practical Information

When We’re There
June 6-9: Late spring/early summer - Weather: High 80s°F (28-30°C), sunny - Crowds: Moderate to high (summer tourist season) - Sunset: ~9:00 PM (long evenings!)


Museum Hours
Most close Mondays - Plan accordingly!
Operating Hours: - Usually 8:15 AM - 6:50 PM (last entry varies) - Some close earlier on weekdays - Check specific museum websites


Money
Budget Per Day (Per Person): - Lodging: €50-100 (shared room) - Food: €40-60 (breakfast, lunch, dinner) - Museums: €20-30 (with advance bookings) - Misc: €10-20 - Total: €120-210 per day
Firenze Card: €85 for 72 hours, unlimited museum entry + skip lines - Worth it if visiting 4+ major museums in 3 days


Safety
Very Safe City: - Low violent crime - Watch for pickpockets in tourist areas - Keep valuables zipped/secure - Don’t leave bags unattended
Scams to Avoid: - “Free” bracelet sellers (aggressive, demand payment) - Restaurant touts (go where Italians eat) - Unlicensed tour guides

title: Florence Overview
text:
Tips for Florence

Beat the Crowds
Book early entry (8:15 AM): - Uffizi - Accademia - Duomo dome climb
Late afternoon (after 4 PM): - Smaller museums - Churches
Avoid weekends if possible (but we can’t control our dates)


Museum Strategy
Don’t try to see everything: - Pick 2-3 must-see pieces per museum - Focus on quality, not quantity - Take breaks (museum fatigue is real)
Pace Yourself: - Max 2 major museums per day - Alternate museum days with outdoor days - Rest when tired (there’s always tomorrow)


Dress Code
Churches: - Shoulders and knees covered - Bring scarf to cover up - They WILL turn you away if improperly dressed
Museums: - No restrictions (casual fine)


Best Times
Morning (8-10 AM): - Quiet streets - Locals at cafés - Museums less crowded
Late Afternoon (4-7 PM): - Aperitivo hour (drink + free snacks) - Museums emptying out - Good light for photos
Evening (8-10 PM): - Dinner time - Piazzas come alive - Street performers - Gelato walks

title: Puglia Overview
text:
About Puglia
La Puglia - The heel of Italy’s boot, stretching 250 miles down the Adriatic coast. Italy’s best-kept secret: stunning coastline, unique architecture, incredible food, authentic culture, and far fewer tourists than Tuscany or the Amalfi Coast.
Our Time Here: 6 days (June 14-19) on organized tour
Why Puglia? - UNESCO trulli houses (unique cone-shaped buildings) - Lecce’s baroque architecture (“Florence of the South”) - Dramatic Adriatic coastline - Authentic southern Italian culture - Exceptional food and wine - Whitewashed hill towns - Off the beaten tourist path

title: Puglia Overview
text:
Trip Context

Arrival (June 13)
Train from Naples to Brindisi (~5-6 hours), scenic journey through southern Italy. Overnight in Brindisi, tour begins June 14.


Tour Days (June 14-19)
6 full days exploring Puglia with organized tour (details to be confirmed): - Expected highlights: Alberobello, Lecce, Polignano a Mare, Ostuni - Mix of coastal and inland towns - Cultural sites and natural beauty - Regional cuisine and wine experiences


Departure (June 20)
Early morning travel from Puglia to Rome Fiumicino airport for departure flight to Madison.

title: Puglia Overview
text:
Puglia at a Glance

Geography
The Heel of the Boot: - 250-mile long region along Adriatic Sea - Mostly flat (unlike mountainous northern Italy) - Endless olive groves (40% of Italy’s olive oil production) - Dramatic limestone coastline - Hot, dry climate (similar to Greece)


History
Ancient Crossroads: - Greek colonies (Magna Graecia, 8th century BC) - Roman conquest and agricultural development - Byzantine influence (Eastern Orthodox traditions) - Norman, Swabian, Spanish rule (Frederick II built Castel del Monte) - Always a bridge between East and West
Result: Unique culture blending Italian, Greek, Byzantine, and Norman influences.


Character
Why It Feels Different: - Less touristy than northern Italy - More Greek/Mediterranean than typically Italian - Rural, agricultural traditions still strong - Slower pace of life - Locals genuinely surprised and pleased to see visitors - Pride in regional identity (Pugliese first, Italian second)

title: Puglia Overview
text:
Expected Tour Highlights
Based on typical Puglia tours, we expect to visit:

Alberobello ⭐ UNESCO World Heritage
The Trulli Capital
What Are Trulli? - Cone-shaped stone buildings unique to Puglia - Built without mortar (dry stone construction) - Whitewashed walls, conical gray stone roofs - Originally built to avoid taxes (could be quickly dismantled when tax collectors came) - Now UNESCO protected
What to See: - Rione Monti (1,000+ trulli, densest concentration) - Rione Aia Piccola (more residential, fewer tourists) - Trullo Sovrano (two-story trullo, now museum) - Church of Sant’Antonio (trullo-style church)
Experience: - Walk through trulli streets - Visit shops in converted trulli - Possible overnight stay IN a trullo - Wine tasting (some trulli are wineries)


Lecce ⭐ “Florence of the South”
Baroque Masterpiece
Why It’s Special: - 17th-century baroque architecture in golden limestone - Incredibly ornate churches and palaces - Compact, walkable historic center - Less crowded than Florence but equally beautiful
What to See: - Piazza del Duomo - One of Italy’s most beautiful squares - Basilica di Santa Croce - Baroque facade covered in sculptures - Roman Amphitheater - 2nd century AD, still partially excavated - Castello Carlo V - 16th-century fortress - Artisan workshops (papier-mâché, ceramics)
Experience: - Baroque architecture walking tour - Pasticciotto (custard-filled pastry) at Caffè Alvino - Evening passeggiata (stroll) in piazzas


Polignano a Mare ⭐ Coastal Drama
Cliffside Beauty
Setting: - Town perched on 20-meter limestone cliffs - Dramatic views over Adriatic Sea - Sea caves below - Former fishing village, now charming resort town
What to See: - Historic Center - Narrow streets, whitewashed buildings - Lama Monachile Beach - Dramatic beach between cliffs - Red Bull Cliff Diving - Location of annual competition - Grotta Palazzese - Restaurant inside sea cave (very expensive)
Experience: - Cliff-top walk with sea views - Swimming (if weather permits) - Seafood lunch overlooking Adriatic - Gelato in historic center


Ostuni - “La Città Bianca” (The White City)
Hilltop Marvel
Appearance: - Whitewashed buildings cascading down hillside - Labyrinth of narrow streets and staircases - Cathedral at the summit - Surrounded by olive groves stretching to sea
What to See: - Historic Center - Explore maze of white streets - Cathedral of Ostuni - Gothic-Romanesque facade - Panoramic Views - Over olive groves to Adriatic - Medieval Walls - Partially intact defensive walls
Experience: - Getting lost in the white maze (intentionally!) - Olive oil tasting - Photography (stunning at any time of day) - Aperitivo with a view


Other Possible Stops
Tours often include some of these:
Matera (Technically Basilicata, but often included) - Sassi di Matera - Ancient cave dwellings, UNESCO site - Continuously inhabited for 9,000 years - Used in films (The Passion of the Christ, No Time to Die)
Castel del Monte ⭐ UNESCO - Frederick II’s mysterious octagonal castle (1240) - Perfect mathematical proportions - Isolated hilltop location - Unknown original purpose
Otranto - Easternmost town in Italy - Byzantine mosaics in cathedral - Turquoise Adriatic waters - Albania visible on clear days
Locorotondo - Circular hilltop town - White houses, narrow streets - Valle d’Itria views
Monopoli - Fishing port with old town - Less touristy than Polignano - Authentic harbor life
Coastal Areas - Beaches along Adriatic coast - Sea caves and grottoes - Swimming opportunities

title: Puglia Overview
text:
Puglia Food & Wine

Regional Specialties
Pasta: - Orecchiette - “Little ears” shaped pasta (Puglia’s signature) - Orecchiette con cime di rapa (with turnip greens, garlic, anchovies) - Cavatelli with tomato sauce
Seafood: - Fresh from Adriatic daily - Grilled fish, octopus, mussels - Sea urchin (ricci di mare) - Riso, patate e cozze (rice, potatoes, mussels - baked)
Meat: - Bombette - Meat rolls stuffed with cheese - Gnummareddi - Lamb offal rolls - Grilled meats (Pugliese love their BBQ)
Cheese: - Burrata - Creamy mozzarella (INVENTED in Puglia!) - Burrata di Andria (the original) - Caciocavallo - Ricotta forte (aged, strong flavor)
Bread & Baked Goods: - Focaccia Barese - With tomatoes and olives - Friselle - Hard bread rings (soak in water, top with tomatoes) - Panzerotti - Fried half-moon calzones - Pasticciotto - Custard-filled pastry (Lecce specialty) - Rustico Leccese - Puff pastry with mozzarella and béchamel


Wine
Puglia = Italy’s Wine Cellar (produces more wine than any other region)
Red Wines: - Primitivo - Full-bodied, fruity (related to California Zinfandel) - Negroamaro - “Black bitter,” smooth, cherry notes - Primitivo di Manduria - DOC designation, premium - Salice Salentino - Blend, very drinkable
White Wines: - Verdeca - Crisp, citrusy - Locorotondo - Fresh, light - Fiano Minutolo - Aromatic
Rosé: - Rosato del Salento - Perfect with seafood


Olive Oil
Puglia produces 40% of Italy’s olive oil
What to Know: - Ancient olive trees (some 1,000+ years old) - DOP designations (Terra di Bari, Dauno, etc.) - Tastings often included in tours - Buy bottles to bring home (pack carefully!)

title: Puglia Overview
text:
Practical Information

When We’re There
June 14-19: Early summer - Weather: High 80s°F (28-30°C), sunny, dry - Coastal breezes make it more comfortable than Naples - Long days (sunset ~8:30 PM) - Peak season starting (but less touristy than Tuscany)


Getting Around
Tour Transportation: - Likely by tour bus or van - All inter-town transport included - May rotate accommodations or stay in one base
Within Towns: - Walking (most historic centers pedestrianized) - Hills and stairs (Ostuni especially) - Cobblestones (again, good shoes!)


Language
Pugliese Dialect: - Very different from standard Italian - Greek and Albanian influences - Locals speak Italian with tourists - English less common than Florence/Rome
Useful: - “Buongiorno” goes a long way - Attempt Italian - locals appreciate effort - Many tour guides bilingual


Money
Budget TBD (depends on tour package): - Tour likely includes: accommodations, some meals, transportation, guides - You’ll pay for: additional meals, drinks, souvenirs, personal expenses
Expected Daily: €30-50 for meals not included, wine, gelato, etc.


Safety
Very Safe: - Low crime - Smaller towns feel even safer than cities - Watch belongings in tourist areas (standard precaution)

title: Flights to Italy
section: Arriving in Pisa: Stay or Go?
text:
Arriving in Pisa: Stay or Go?
You have two good options when landing at Pisa Galileo Galilei Airport (PSA):

Option A: Head Straight to Florence (Recommended)







Step
Details




From Airport
Take the PisaMover shuttle (8 min, automated) to Pisa Centrale station


Train
Direct Regionale to Firenze Santa Maria Novella (SMN)


Duration
50–70 minutes depending on service


Frequency
Every 30 minutes, ~52 trains per day


Price
€10–€12 one way (fixed price, no advance booking needed)


First Train
4:15 AM from Pisa Centrale


Last Train
9:01 PM (arrives Florence 10:26 PM)



This is the most efficient option. You’ll be in Florence within 2 hours of clearing customs. The train drops you at SMN station, right in the historic center.


Option B: Overnight in Pisa
If you arrive late or want to ease into the trip, spending one night in Pisa is pleasant. The city is charming beyond the Leaning Tower:
Why Stay: - Piazza dei Miracoli (with the Tower, Cathedral, and Baptistery) is genuinely spectacular - Arno riverfront at sunset is lovely - See Pisa’s highlights in a leisurely evening and morning - Reduce travel stress after long flight
Where to Stay: - Stay near the train station or along the Arno — not near the Tower (touristy and overpriced) - Hotel Bologna or Royal Victoria are good mid-range picks
What to See: - Leaning Tower and Piazza dei Miracoli complex (allow 2-3 hours) - Walk along the Arno - Quiet piazzas away from the tourist center

title: Flights to Italy
text:
Return Flight: June 20, 2026

Rome (FCO) → Chicago/Madison
Departure: Early morning from Rome Fiumicino Airport (FCO)
Key Considerations: - Getting from Puglia to Rome the night before or very early morning - Travel time from Brindisi/Puglia to Rome: ~4-6 hours by train - Recommendation: Travel to Rome on June 19 evening and stay near FCO airport for early June 20 departure


Airport Logistics
Rome Fiumicino (FCO): - Larger international hub than Pisa - More direct return flight options to Chicago - Airport hotels available for overnight before early flight - Express train to city center (Leonardo Express, 32 min to Termini)

title: Flights to Italy
text:
Booking Strategy

When to Book
Timeline: - 2-3 months ahead: Best balance of price and availability - Too early: Prices often haven’t dropped to competitive levels - Too late: Limited availability, higher prices
For our June 4 departure: Book by late March/early April 2026


Booking Checklist

Compare prices on Google Flights, airline sites, and SkyLux
Check baggage allowances (especially for souvenirs on return)
Select seats early (aisle vs. window preference)
Add to calendar with confirmation numbers
Print or download boarding passes 24 hours before
Notify credit cards of international travel dates
Check passport validity (must be valid 6 months beyond return)



Seat Selection Tips
Transatlantic Leg: - Business/First: Window for sleep, aisle for movement - Economy: Aisle for long-haul comfort and bathroom access - Avoid seats near lavatories and galleys (noise) - Emergency exit rows: More legroom but no under-seat storage during takeoff/landing
European Connection: - Usually shorter flights (1-2 hours) - Less critical but still book early for best selection

title: Flights to Italy

Luggage Considerations

Checked Baggage
Typical Allowances: - Economy: 1-2 bags, 50 lbs each (varies by airline and fare class) - Business: 2-3 bags, 70 lbs each - First: 3 bags, 70 lbs each
Strategy: - Check allowances for specific booking - Leave room in checked bags for return (wine, art, souvenirs) - Consider shipping heavy items if over limit


Carry-On
Standard: 1 carry-on + 1 personal item
Pack in Carry-On: - Medications - Valuables (jewelry, electronics) - Change of clothes (in case checked bag delayed) - Travel documents - Essential toiletries (3.4 oz / 100ml limit)

Baggage: 2 × 23kg checked + 1 × 8kg carry-on per person on both flights






NoteWhy via Rome?



There are no direct commercial flights between Naples (NAP) and Brindisi (BDS) — the route is too short and thin for direct service. Routing via Rome Fiumicino (FCO) is the only way to fly this leg. The total journey time (including connection) is approximately 3.5 hours door-to-door from central Naples — comparable to the 5.5-hour direct train option that was the original plan.

text:
